answer: Form data can be used on the client-side to immediately update the interface or sent to a web server for processing and storage. You would choose to use client-side processing when you want to update the interface immediately, such as adding another item to a list or showing/hiding a UI feature, and choose server-side processing when you need to store or process the data on the server, such as sending a contact message.

The `<input type='email'>` field differs from a standard `<input type='text'>` field in that it performs some validation checks on the data typed by the user to ensure it is a well-formed email address. This provides a better user experience. On mobile devices, it also causes a more appropriate keyboard layout for entering email addresses to appear, such as one with an @ symbol by default.

submit, reset, button. 
A click on a submit button sends the form's data to the web page defined by the action attribute of the <form> element. 
A click on a reset button resets all the form widgets to their default value immediately, which is considered bad practice from a UX point of view. 
A click on a button button does nothing, but it's useful for building custom buttons with JavaScript.

1. text fields
2. dropdown boxes
3. buttons
4. checkboxes
5. radio buttons
6. <input> element
7. <textarea> element 
8. <button> element
